nodes.name: builder
nodes.description: Worker agent that executes ONE scoped implementation task. Writes and edits code, runs shell commands. Does not spawn subagents, does not expand scope. Use for bounded build/implement/fix tasks delegated from an orchestrator.

nodes.name: code-reviewer
nodes.description: Read-only code reviewer. Use proactively after any implementation task is complete. Checks correctness, security, spec alignment, and test coverage. Applies self-challenge rubrics and issues an explicit APPROVE or BLOCK decision with evidence-backed findings grouped by severity.

nodes.name: context-curator
nodes.description: Curates memory context for worker agents. Given a task description, selects relevant memories from Valkey and Obsidian and returns a <context-memory> block. Does NOT perform tasks.

nodes.name: developer
nodes.description: Developer Agent in a multi-agent system

nodes.name: documenter
nodes.description: Generates documentation for completed and validated features.

nodes.name: explore
nodes.description: Read-only exploration agent. Surveys codebases, reads Jira tickets, and searches Confluence docs to build context for planning. Never modifies files.

nodes.name: glide-code-reviewer
nodes.description: Specialized GLIDE code reviewer. Reviews Valkey GLIDE client code for correctness, anti-patterns, resource leaks, and cluster-awareness using the glide skill references. Invoked as a subagent of code-reviewer when GLIDE code is in the diff.

nodes.name: research-summarizer
nodes.description: Orchestrates research, validation, and produces a final summary

nodes.name: research-validator
nodes.description: Adversarial verification agent. Cross-checks researcher findings against cited source URLs using firecrawl. Classifies each claim as CONFIRMED, UNVERIFIED, or CONTRADICTED. Use after the researcher agent to validate accuracy before passing findings to the caller.

nodes.name: researcher
nodes.description: Search external documentation, APIs, and libraries to answer a specific question. Use proactively when a caller needs citable external knowledge before implementing or deciding. Returns concise findings with source URLs, tradeoffs, and a concrete recommendation.

nodes.name: security-reviewer
nodes.description: Threat-model-driven security analysis anchored to CWE taxonomy. Injection, access control, secrets, crypto, SSRF, path traversal. Read-only - never modifies files.

nodes.name: superhuman
nodes.description: Expert engineer with deep domain knowledge in AWS, backend, UI/UX, and architecture. Use proactively for complex, multi-step engineering tasks requiring deep reasoning. Uses sequential thinking for complex problem-solving and firecrawl for research. Cross-checks all solutions for correctness.

nodes.name: team-lead
nodes.description: Team orchestrator that creates plans and delegates work to specialized subagents (builder, validator).

nodes.name: team-leader
nodes.description: Orchestrator. Delegates tasks, tracks TODO. Does NOT write code or run commands.

nodes.name: tester
nodes.description: Test and verification agent. Runs tests, linters, and security scans. Reports commands, pass/fail status, and repro steps for failures. Writes new tests when coverage is missing. Recommends merge only when all quality gates pass.

nodes.name: validator
nodes.description: Verify that a completed implementation task satisfies its acceptance criteria. Read-only — inspects files, runs checks, and issues a scored PASS or FAIL report. Use after the builder agent finishes a bounded task.

nodes.name: valkey-glide-implementor
nodes.description: Generates production-ready Valkey GLIDE code snippets. Uses the glide skill to produce accurate, working code for client setup, vector search, batch operations, caching, and session management across all 6 supported languages.

nodes.name: brainstorming
nodes.description: You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation.
nodes.excerpt: Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue. Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get u

nodes.name: code-review-excellence
nodes.description: Reasoning framework for systematic code review. Provides lenses (correctness, security, design fit, testability), self-challenge rubrics, and severity differentiation. Use as the reasoning frame when a reviewer subagent applies multi-lens analysis to a diff or PR. Reports only findings affecting correctness or stated requirements — explicitly excludes style.
nodes.excerpt: Code Review Excellence Transform code reviews from gatekeeping to knowledge sharing through constructive feedback, systematic analysis, and collaborative improvement. When to Use This Skill - Reviewing pull requests and code changes - Establishing code review standards for teams - Mentoring junior d

nodes.name: create-skill
nodes.description: Use when the user wants to create a new skill for their AI agents
nodes.excerpt: Create Skill Create a new skill across all synced roots. **Entity type:** Skill **Sync convention:** Follow `_shared/three-root-sync.md` (paths, sync rules, and workflow pattern for entity type "Skill"). For create operations, also check for naming conflicts across all three directories before writi

nodes.name: diagram-valkey-flow
nodes.description: Analyze a project and generate a Mermaid sequence diagram showing the full data flow from the application layer through any framework/middleware down to Valkey operations. Works for any language or framework.
nodes.excerpt: Diagram Valkey Flow Analyze the project at `$ARGUMENTS` (or the current working directory if blank) and produce a Mermaid sequence diagram showing how data flows from the application entry point through to Valkey. Run all phases in order. Do not skip phases. --- Phase 1: Discover Entry Points and Fr

nodes.name: dispatching-parallel-agents
nodes.description: Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies
nodes.excerpt: Dispatching Parallel Agents Overview You delegate tasks to specialized agents with isolated context. By precisely crafting their instructions and context, you ensure they stay focused and succeed at their task. They should never inherit your session's context or history — you construct exactly what

nodes.name: executing-plans
nodes.description: Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute in a separate session with review checkpoints
nodes.excerpt: Executing Plans Overview Load plan, review critically, execute all tasks, report when complete. **Announce at start:** "I'm using the executing-plans skill to implement this plan." **Note:** Tell your human partner that Superpowers works much better with access to subagents. The quality of its work

nodes.name: find-skills
nodes.description: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
nodes.excerpt: Find Skills This skill helps you discover and install skills from the open agent skills ecosystem. When to Use This Skill Use this skill when the user: - Asks "how do I do X" where X might be a common task with an existing skill - Says "find a skill for X" or "is there a skill for X" - Asks "can you

nodes.name: finishing-a-development-branch
nodes.description: Use when implementation is complete, all tests pass, and you need to decide how to integrate the work - guides completion of development work by presenting structured options for merge, PR, or cleanup
nodes.excerpt: Finishing a Development Branch Overview Guide completion of development work by presenting clear options and handling chosen workflow. **Core principle:** Verify tests → Detect environment → Present options → Execute choice → Clean up. **Announce at start:** "I'm using the finishing-a-development-br

nodes.name: glide
nodes.description: Production-ready patterns for Valkey GLIDE clients across 6 languages. Activate when generating, reviewing, or debugging Valkey GLIDE code including client creation, batch/pipeline/transaction operations, clustering, authentication, TLS, error handling, and timeout configuration.
nodes.excerpt: **Based on valkey-glide v2.4.0** ⚠️ CRITICAL: Version Verification **BEFORE writing or reviewing any GLIDE code, determine the target GLIDE version.** Detect version from: `package.json` (`@valkey/valkey-glide`), `requirements.txt`/`pyproject.toml` (`valkey-glide`), `pom.xml`/`build.gradle` (`io.val

nodes.name: humanizer
nodes.description:
Remove signs of AI-generated writing from text. Use when editing or reviewing
text to make it sound more natural and human-written. Based on Wikipedia's
comprehensive "Signs of AI writing" guide. Detects and fixes patterns including:
inflated symbolism, promotional language, superficial -ing analyses, vague
attributions, em dash overuse, rule of three, AI vocabulary words, passive
voice, negative parallelisms, and filler phrases.
nodes.excerpt: Humanizer: Remove AI Writing Patterns You are a writing editor that identifies and removes signs of AI-generated text to make writing sound more natural and human. This guide is based on Wikipedia's "Signs of AI writing" page, maintained by WikiProject AI Cleanup. Your Task When given text to humani

nodes.name: implement-cookbook
nodes.description: Given a PR link containing a Valkey integration in a project, create a cookbook matching the style of valkey-io/Valkey-Samples (you can find it in ~/valkey/Valkey-Samples). Builds the cookbook locally in the project directory, verifies it runs, then copies to ~/valkey/valkey-samples/ and removes the local copy. Use when asked to create a cookbook, write a cookbook, or implement a cookbook from a PR. Also works from local git commits on the current branch.
nodes.excerpt: Implement Cookbook Produce a working cookbook that demonstrates the Valkey integration in the current project. --- Phase 1: Analyze the Integration Determine the input source: - If `$ARGUMENTS` is a PR link: fetch the PR diff and description using GitHub tools. - If `$ARGUMENTS` is empty or not a UR

nodes.name: implement-jira
nodes.description: Implement a Jira task end-to-end — fetches requirements, scans the codebase, plans with Opus, implements with Sonnet (delegating low-complexity subtasks to Haiku), runs tests with a fix loop, then runs a merged code review. Uses Valkey at localhost:8888 as a two-tier cache. Use when given a Jira issue key (e.g. FOO-123). Do NOT use for tasks without a Jira ticket, quick fixes, or exploratory coding — handle those directly.
nodes.excerpt: Implement Jira Task Implement the Jira task `$ARGUMENTS` end-to-end using the workflow below. Follow each phase in order. Do not skip phases. This workflow assigns specific models to each subagent for cost efficiency, and uses a two-tier cache (Anthropic prompt caching + Valkey at `localhost:8888`)

nodes.name: multi-discipline-review
nodes.description: Run a parallel multi-discipline review across codebase alignment, correctness, security, testability, and Valkey GLIDE using dedicated sub-agents (code-reviewer, security-reviewer, tester, glide-code-reviewer). Codebase alignment is the primary lens. Findings are merged and validated by an Opus validator. Use when a single-lens review is insufficient for a PR or diff.
nodes.excerpt: Multi-Discipline Code Review `$ARGUMENTS`: diff, file path, branch name, or PR reference. Phase 1: Gather Context Read the code, language, framework, existing patterns, and any linked requirements. **Size gating — determine review depth:** - Count `additions + deletions` in the diff. - If `<= 150 li

nodes.name: pr-comment-humanizer
nodes.description:
Humanize PR review comments to match the author's terse, direct code review voice.
Strips AI-isms and rewrites comments to sound like a real engineer: short imperatives,
parenthetical precision, "nit" prefix for minor things, "we should" for collaborative
framing, questions to verify rather than assert, links instead of explanations.
nodes.excerpt: PR Comment Humanizer Rewrite PR review comment text to match this specific voice: terse, direct, imperative. No warmup, no hedging, no AI vocabulary. Voice Profile Derived from real PR comments at https://github.com/edlng/ChatDev/pull/1. **Core traits:** - **Brutally terse.** If one line does it, us

nodes.name: receiving-code-review
nodes.description: Use when receiving code review feedback, before implementing suggestions, especially if feedback seems unclear or technically questionable - requires technical rigor and verification, not performative agreement or blind implementation
nodes.excerpt: Code Review Reception Overview Code review requires technical evaluation, not emotional performance. **Core principle:** Verify before implementing. Ask before assuming. Technical correctness over social comfort. The Response Pattern ``` WHEN receiving code review feedback: 1. READ: Complete feedbac

nodes.name: requesting-code-review
nodes.description: Use when completing tasks, implementing major features, or before merging to verify work meets requirements
nodes.excerpt: Requesting Code Review Dispatch a code reviewer subagent to catch issues before they cascade. The reviewer gets precisely crafted context for evaluation — never your session's history. This keeps the reviewer focused on the work product, not your thought process, and preserves your own context for c

nodes.name: review-addressed-comments
nodes.description: Given a PR link, reviews edlng's review comments, verifies each was addressed in the latest code, and provides a final verdict (APPROVE, REQUEST CHANGES, or COMMENT) with code evidence.
nodes.excerpt: Review Addressed Comments Verify that all of edlng's review comments on a PR have been addressed in the current code, providing the new code snippets as evidence, and give a final recommendation. When to Use - When asked to check whether edlng's review feedback has been addressed on a PR - Before ap

nodes.name: review-code
nodes.description: Self-review uncommitted or unpushed work before opening a PR. Multi-phase review with Jira requirements, Valkey caching, a single Sonnet reviewer, an Opus validator skeptic pass, and auto-fix offer. Pass a branch name to review vs main, a Jira key to use as requirements source, or leave empty to review staged+unstaged changes.
nodes.excerpt: Review My Local Changes Self-review your own uncommitted or unpushed work before opening a PR. The goal is to catch your own mistakes — bugs, sloppy patterns, missed requirements — at the cheapest possible point. Optimized for speed and **fixing in place**: when a finding is unambiguous, this comman

nodes.name: review-cookbook-pr
nodes.description: Review a cookbook pull request. This is the standard PR review **plus** two cookbook-specific checks.
nodes.excerpt: Review Cookbook PR (senior-dev grade) Review a cookbook pull request. This is the standard PR review **plus** two cookbook-specific checks. Run the base workflow Follow `_shared/pr-review-base.md` (relative path: `../_shared/pr-review-base.md`) for cache setup, Phase 1 (context), Phase 2 (merged-len

nodes.name: review-pr
nodes.description: Senior-grade PR review against the linked Jira ticket and existing codebase. Parallel Sonnet reviewers per discipline, findings merged and validated by a single Opus validator pass. Output is local-only — printed in chat and saved to Obsidian, never posted to GitHub.
nodes.excerpt: Review PR (senior-dev grade) Review someone else's pull request against the linked Jira ticket and the existing codebase. This is the **standard** PR review. The full workflow is defined in the shared base file so it can be reused by specialized variants (e.g. `review-cookbook-pr`). Run the base wor

nodes.name: subagent-driven-development
nodes.description: Use when executing implementation plans with independent tasks in the current session
nodes.excerpt: Subagent-Driven Development Execute plan by dispatching fresh subagent per task, with two-stage review after each: spec compliance review first, then code quality review. **Why subagents:** You delegate tasks to specialized agents with isolated context. By precisely crafting their instructions and c

nodes.name: systematic-debugging
nodes.description: Use when encountering any bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior, before proposing fixes
nodes.excerpt: Systematic Debugging Overview Random fixes waste time and create new bugs. Quick patches mask underlying issues. **Core principle:** ALWAYS find root cause before attempting fixes. Symptom fixes are failure. **Violating the letter of this process is violating the spirit of debugging.** The Iron Law

nodes.name: test-driven-development
nodes.description: Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code
nodes.excerpt: Test-Driven Development (TDD) Overview Write the test first. Watch it fail. Write minimal code to pass. **Core principle:** If you didn't watch the test fail, you don't know if it tests the right thing. **Violating the letter of the rules is violating the spirit of the rules.** When to Use **Always:

nodes.name: test-valkey
nodes.description: Test Valkey integration code against a live valkey-bundle container. Starts Valkey from a clean slate, exercises staged/unstaged git changes (cache backend, vector search provider, rate limiter, connection parsing), validates all configurations by tearing down and recreating the container as needed. Use when Valkey integration code has been written and needs end-to-end verification before commit.
nodes.excerpt: Test Valkey Run integration tests against the staged/unstaged Valkey changes in the current repository. Proves correctness by exercising the code against a real `valkey/valkey-bundle` container. --- Prerequisites Before starting, verify: ```bash Container runtime podman -v 2>/dev/null || docker -v 2

nodes.name: update-agent
nodes.description: Use when the user wants to update or modify an existing agent definition for their AI agents

Additional guidance - Agent files are flat markdown (no subdirectory structure). The syn

Additional guidance - If the skill references `_shared/` files, check whether the change belongs in

nodes.name: using-git-worktrees
nodes.description: Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans - ensures an isolated workspace exists via native tools or git worktree fallback
nodes.excerpt: Using Git Worktrees Overview Ensure work happens in an isolated workspace. Prefer your platform's native worktree tools. Fall back to manual git worktrees only when no native tool is available. **Core principle:** Detect existing isolation first. Then use native tools. Then fall back to git. Never f

nodes.name: using-superpowers
nodes.description: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
nodes.excerpt: <SUBAGENT-STOP> If you were dispatched as a subagent to execute a specific task, skip this skill. </SUBAGENT-STOP> <EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT> If you think there is even a 1% chance a skill might apply to what you are doing, you ABSOLUTELY MUST invoke the skill. IF A SKILL APPLIES TO YOUR TASK, YOU DO NOT

nodes.name: valkey-spike
nodes.description: Analyze a repository for Valkey integration opportunities and empirically verify feasibility. Combines static codebase analysis (existing Redis/cache usage, command compatibility, vector search potential) with a live spike against a running Valkey instance to prove the riskiest assumptions before committing to a full implementation. Use when evaluating whether a project is a good candidate for Valkey integration.
nodes.excerpt: Valkey Spike Analyze the repository `$ARGUMENTS` (or the current working directory if blank) for Valkey integration opportunities, then **prove the riskiest assumptions work** against a live Valkey instance. This is a four-phase workflow: 1. **Evidence Collection** - scan the codebase 2. **Compatibi

nodes.name: verification-before-completion
nodes.description: Use when about to claim work is complete, fixed, or passing, before committing or creating PRs - requires running verification commands and confirming output before making any success claims; evidence before assertions always
nodes.excerpt: Verification Before Completion Overview Claiming work is complete without verification is dishonesty, not efficiency. **Core principle:** Evidence before claims, always. **Violating the letter of this rule is violating the spirit of this rule.** The Iron Law ``` NO COMPLETION CLAIMS WITHOUT FRESH VE

nodes.name: write-narrative
nodes.description: Given a Jira issue key, fetch the issue, research the topic with a research subagent, and draft a humanized technical narrative. Saves to ~/Documents/work/narratives/ and outputs in chat. Use when asked to write a narrative, technical assessment, or compatibility analysis for a Jira ticket. Do NOT use for PR descriptions (use write-pr), code reviews, or general documentation.
nodes.excerpt: Write Narrative Given a Jira issue key in `$ARGUMENTS` (e.g. `AEA-468`), fetch the issue, research the topic, and draft a Narrative for me and send it in the chat, and also create the file (see Phase 4). Follow each phase in order. Do not skip phases. --- Phase 1: Fetch Jira Issue Use `mcp__atlassia

nodes.name: write-pr
nodes.description: Generate a polished, human-sounding PR description from the current git changes. Optionally accepts a commit range (e.g. HEAD~3) and/or a PR template path. Reads the diff, drafts from the repo's PR template (or a default), verifies accuracy, then humanizes the output.
nodes.excerpt: Write PR Description Generate a polished, human-sounding PR description for the current changes. Follow each phase in order. Do not skip phases. Input `$ARGUMENTS` is optional and may contain: 1. A **commit range** (e.g. `HEAD~3`, `main..HEAD`). Default: the last commit plus any staged/unstaged chan

nodes.name: write-pr-comments
nodes.description: Post inline PR review comments from an Obsidian review note as a pending review. Filters out non-actionable findings, presents each remaining one for approval, then posts approved comments as a pending GitHub review (visible only to you until manually submitted).
nodes.excerpt: Write PR Comments Post actionable review findings as inline GitHub PR comments from a previously saved Obsidian review note. `$ARGUMENTS` is a PR URL, `owner/repo#number`, an Obsidian note slug (e.g. `valkey-samples-feat-add-eino-...`), or empty. If a slug is given, read the note directly from `PRs/

nodes.name: writing-plans
nodes.description: Use when you have a spec or requirements for a multi-step task, before touching code
nodes.excerpt: Writing Plans Overview Write comprehensive implementation plans assuming the engineer has zero context for our codebase and questionable taste. Document everything they need to know: which files to touch for each task, code, testing, docs they might need to check, how to test it. Give them the whole

nodes.name: writing-skills
nodes.description: Use when creating new skills, editing existing skills, or verifying skills work before deployment
nodes.excerpt: Writing Skills Overview **Writing skills IS Test-Driven Development applied to process documentation.** **Personal skills live in agent-specific directories (`~/.claude/skills` for Claude Code, `~/.agents/skills/` for Codex)** You write test cases (pressure scenarios with subagents), watch them fail

nodes.name: async-dispatch-protocols
nodes.description: Shared reference for agents that delegate work asynchronously (assign/fire-and-forget) and receive results via inbox delivery.

nodes.name: codebase-context-checklist
nodes.description: Shared reference used by `review-pr`, `review-cookbook-pr`, `review-code`, and `implement-jira`. Not a standalone skill. Single source of truth for what to capture when scanning a codebase before reviewing or implementing.

nodes.name: humanizer-rules
nodes.description: Shared reference for humanizer-based skills. Contains the AI writing patterns to detect and strip. Not a standalone skill.

nodes.name: memory-protocol
nodes.description: Shared reference for agents that store or consume persistent memory. Defines scopes, storage backends, injection format, and conventions.

nodes.name: no-github-writes
nodes.description: Shared reference used by the local-only review skills (`review-pr`, `review-cookbook-pr`, `review-code`). Not a standalone skill. Single source of truth for the read-only output rule.

nodes.name: pr-review-base
nodes.description: Shared reference used by `review-pr` and `review-cookbook-pr`. Not a standalone skill. Single source of truth for the senior-grade PR review workflow (context gathering, merged-lens review, validator pass, local report). Consuming skills invoke this base and add their own specialized phases (e.g. cookbook-specific checks).

nodes.name: review-findings-schema
nodes.description: Shared reference used by `review-pr`, `review-cookbook-pr`, and `review-code`. Not a standalone skill. Single source of truth for the findings JSON schema, the review lenses, and the filing rules. Consuming skills may add skill-specific lenses or severity vocabularies on top of this base.

nodes.name: security-constraints
nodes.description: Standardized security boundary for all agents. Reference from agent prompts with: "See `_shared/security-constraints.md`."

nodes.name: three-root-sync
nodes.description: Shared reference used by `update-skill`, `update-agent`, and `create-skill`. Not a standalone skill. Single source of truth for the three-location sync rule and the paths involved.

nodes.name: validator-skeptic-pass
nodes.description: Shared reference used by `review-pr`, `review-cookbook-pr`, `review-code`, and `multi-discipline-review`. Not a standalone skill. Single source of truth for the adversarial validator pass that maximizes signal by killing false positives. Consuming skills choose the model/subagent role and may add skill-specific self-challenge questions.

nodes.name: valkey-cache-conventions
nodes.description: This is a shared reference file used by multiple skills (`review-pr`, `review-cookbook-pr`, `review-code`, `implement-jira`). It is not a standalone skill. Do not edit one skill's copy in isolation — this file is the single source of truth for how those skills use Valkey caching.

nodes.name: github
nodes.description: MCP server: github

nodes.name: obsidian
nodes.description: MCP server: obsidian

nodes.name: cao-mcp-server
nodes.description: MCP server: cao-mcp-server

nodes.name: firecrawl
nodes.description: MCP server: firecrawl

nodes.name: sequential-thinking
nodes.description: MCP server: sequential-thinking

workflows.name: Team Lead Pipeline
workflows.description: Full implementation workflow from spec to merged code. Context and research run in parallel before each build; both review stages can loop back.

nodes.label: Explore
nodes.description: Survey codebase: existing patterns, relevant files, interfaces, conventions

nodes.label: Plan
nodes.description: Extract all tasks from spec findings, create TODO, classify complexity

nodes.label: Gather Context
nodes.description: Fetch relevant memories from Valkey + Obsidian for the builder

nodes.label: Research
nodes.description: Investigate unfamiliar APIs/libraries before implementation

nodes.label: Build
nodes.description: Implement the task (or superhuman for complex tasks)

nodes.label: Validate Spec
nodes.description: Verify requirements are met - nothing missing, nothing extra

nodes.label: Spec met?
nodes.description: Did the builder satisfy every requirement?

nodes.label: Review Quality
nodes.description: Check correctness, codebase alignment, maintainability

nodes.label: Approved?
nodes.description: Is the code correct and well-built?

nodes.label: Document
nodes.description: Generate documentation for completed features

workflows.name: Research Pipeline
workflows.description: Research a topic with adversarial validation. Unverified findings loop back for another pass; only confirmed findings reach the summary.

nodes.label: Research
nodes.description: Search external docs, APIs, libraries. Returns findings with source URLs.

nodes.label: Validate
nodes.description: Cross-check cited sources. Classify each claim as CONFIRMED/UNVERIFIED/CONTRADICTED.

nodes.label: Confirmed?
nodes.description: Are the findings backed by their cited sources?

nodes.label: Synthesize
nodes.description: Produce final summary using only CONFIRMED findings with citations.

workflows.name: Code Review Pipeline
workflows.description: Multi-phase review with a skeptic validator pass, branching to an APPROVE or BLOCK verdict.

nodes.label: Gather Diff
nodes.description: Collect uncommitted/unpushed changes or PR diff

nodes.label: Multi-lens Review
nodes.description: Review for correctness, security, design fit, testability, performance

nodes.label: Skeptic Pass
nodes.description: Challenge review findings - are they real issues or false positives?

nodes.label: Issues found?
nodes.description: Did the skeptic pass confirm real blocking issues?

nodes.label: Report BLOCK
nodes.description: Aggregate and format findings into BLOCK verdict report

nodes.label: Report APPROVE
nodes.description: Aggregate and format findings into APPROVE verdict report

workflows.name: Jira Implementation
workflows.description: End-to-end implementation from a Jira ticket. Tests loop back to implementation until green.

nodes.label: Fetch Requirements + Scan
nodes.description: Read Jira ticket and survey codebase in one pass

nodes.label: Plan
nodes.description: Break requirements into implementation tasks with dependency ordering

nodes.label: Gather Context
nodes.description: Load relevant memories and prior decisions

nodes.label: Implement
nodes.description: Execute each task (with complexity routing to superhuman if needed)

nodes.label: Test
nodes.description: Run tests and write missing tests

nodes.label: Tests pass?
nodes.description: Are all tests green?

nodes.label: Review
nodes.description: Merged code review across the full implementation

workflows.name: Security Review
workflows.description: Threat-model-driven security analysis anchored to CWE taxonomy.

nodes.label: Threat Model
nodes.description: Identify attack surface: inputs, boundaries, data flows

nodes.label: CWE Analysis
nodes.description: Check for injection, access control, secrets, crypto, SSRF, path traversal

nodes.label: Vulns found?
nodes.description: Did analysis surface any vulnerabilities?

nodes.label: Report Findings
nodes.description: Report vulnerabilities with CWE IDs, severity, and remediation guidance

nodes.label: Report Clear
nodes.description: No blocking vulnerabilities found
